title: Studio launchpad
intro: This is a Studio-only sequence. The browser Practice sandbox teaches Luau; it does not replace Explorer, playtesting, or the client-server runtime.

stations.label: OBSERVE
stations.title: Open the tools that tell the truth
stations.body: In Roblox Studio, open Explorer, Properties, and Output. Explorer shows the DataModel tree; Properties shows the selected Instance; Output is where prints, warnings, and runtime errors become evidence.
checks: Explorer, Properties, and Output are all visible before I begin.
I can distinguish edit-time objects in Explorer from messages produced during a playtest.
I know Practice output is not Roblox Studio Output.

stations.label: MAP
stations.title: Read the live place as a tree
stations.body: A Roblox place is a hierarchy of Instances. Workspace holds the 3D world; ServerScriptService is for server-side logic; ReplicatedStorage is shared storage, not a safe place for secrets. Placement is behavior, not filing preference.
checks: I can locate Workspace and ServerScriptService in Explorer.
I can explain why a Part belongs in Workspace and server game rules belong in ServerScriptService.
I do not treat ReplicatedStorage as private storage.

stations.label: PLACE
stations.title: Choose the script before writing it
stations.body: Use a Script for server work, a LocalScript for client-only work, and a ModuleScript for reusable code. A Script's location and RunContext matter. For this first build, keep the Script in ServerScriptService so the execution side is explicit.
checks: I created a Script under ServerScriptService, not a LocalScript.
I can name one client-only concern, such as local UI or input.
I can name one shared-code concern that belongs in a ModuleScript.

stations.label: BUILD
stations.title: Ship one visible server-side change
stations.body: Create an anchored Part in Workspace and rename it LaunchPad. Put this code in a Script under ServerScriptService, press Play, then use Output and the 3D view to verify the result. Stop the playtest before changing the edit-time place again.
stations.code:
local Workspace = game:GetService("Workspace")
local launchPad = Workspace:WaitForChild("LaunchPad")

launchPad.Anchored = true
launchPad.Material = Enum.Material.Neon
launchPad.Color = Color3.fromRGB(98, 255, 160)
print("LaunchPad configured on the server")
checks: A Part named LaunchPad exists in Workspace before I press Play.
The Script is in ServerScriptService and prints the confirmation once during the playtest.
The part is anchored, neon, and green in the running playtest.
If WaitForChild waits forever, I inspect the Explorer name and placement instead of guessing.

stations.label: REACT
stations.title: Let the server react to a real event
stations.body: A game becomes interactive when code reacts to an event instead of only running once at startup. Extend the same server Script: when a character touches LaunchPad, the server identifies the Player and prints one meaningful event. The short cooldown makes repeated physics contacts observable without flooding Output.
stations.code:
local Players = game:GetService("Players")
local Workspace = game:GetService("Workspace")
local launchPad = Workspace:WaitForChild("LaunchPad")
local onCooldown = {}

launchPad.Touched:Connect(function(hit)
    local character = hit:FindFirstAncestorOfClass("Model")
    local player = character and Players:GetPlayerFromCharacter(character)
    if not player or onCooldown[player] then
        return
    end

    onCooldown[player] = true
    print(player.Name .. " reached LaunchPad")
    task.delay(1, function()
        onCooldown[player] = nil
    end)
end)
checks: I extended the existing Script in ServerScriptService instead of creating a second competing Script.
Walking onto LaunchPad prints my player name in Studio Output.
I can explain that hit is the touched Instance, not automatically a Player.
Repeated contact does not flood Output because the server owns a short cooldown.

stations.label: OWN
stations.title: Separate a player action from game truth
stations.body: The player can cause an interaction, but the server decides whether it changes game state. For the LaunchPad, the server independently receives the touch, resolves the Player, applies its cooldown, and decides what to print. Later, a client may request an action through a RemoteEvent; it still never gets authority to grant coins, unlocks, damage, or purchases.
checks: I can name the request: a player reached LaunchPad.
I can name the server decision: whether this player may trigger the action now.
I would validate any future client-supplied target, amount, or inventory claim on the server.
I do not confuse a LocalScript changing a UI label with changing shared game truth.

stations.label: ROUTE
stations.title: Put input and UI on the client side
stations.body: Input and interface feedback belong close to the player, normally in LocalScripts under client containers such as StarterPlayerScripts or StarterGui. Their job is to turn a player's action into an intent and present feedback. The server remains responsible for shared or valuable state. Do not build a RemoteEvent yet: first be able to state exactly what the request is and what the server will validate.
checks: I can identify input and local interface feedback as client concerns.
I can identify shared rewards, progression, and other valuable state as server concerns.
Before adding a remote, I can write its request, validation rules, and server-owned result in one sentence.

stations.label: CONNECT
stations.title: Create one narrow request channel
stations.body: In Explorer, add a RemoteEvent called LaunchPadRequest under ReplicatedStorage. Both the client and server can see that location, but visibility is not trust. This remote has one narrow meaning: a player requests to activate the one named LaunchPad. It does not carry a reward amount, arbitrary target, or client-decided outcome.
checks: ReplicatedStorage contains exactly one RemoteEvent named LaunchPadRequest.
I can state the request without saying the player grants themselves anything.
I would create a different, purpose-named remote for a different action rather than an all-purpose command remote.

stations.label: VALIDATE
stations.title: Make the server the gatekeeper
stations.body: Replace the earlier touch handler with this server Script. The server checks the request shape, finds the server-side character/root part, checks distance to the server-side LaunchPad, rate-limits the player, then sends only a feedback message. There is deliberately no currency, unlock, or saved data yet.
stations.code:
local Players = game:GetService("Players")
local ReplicatedStorage = game:GetService("ReplicatedStorage")
local Workspace = game:GetService("Workspace")

local request = ReplicatedStorage:WaitForChild("LaunchPadRequest")
local launchPad = Workspace:WaitForChild("LaunchPad")
local lastRequest = {}
local COOLDOWN_SECONDS = 1
local MAX_DISTANCE = 12

request.OnServerEvent:Connect(function(player, action)
    if typeof(action) ~= "string" or action ~= "activate" then
        return
    end

    local character = player.Character
    local root = character and character:FindFirstChild("HumanoidRootPart")
    if not root or (root.Position - launchPad.Position).Magnitude > MAX_DISTANCE then
        request:FireClient(player, "Move closer to LaunchPad")
        return
    end

    local now = time()
    if now - (lastRequest[player] or 0) < COOLDOWN_SECONDS then
        return
    end
    lastRequest[player] = now
    request:FireClient(player, "LaunchPad request accepted")
end)

Players.PlayerRemoving:Connect(function(player)
    lastRequest[player] = nil
end)
checks: The server Script is still under ServerScriptService and gets the RemoteEvent with WaitForChild.
The server rejects any action except the exact string activate.
The server checks the server-side character and distance before accepting the request.
The server keeps and cleans up its own per-player cooldown state.
I can point to the line that makes the server—not the client—the decision maker.

stations.label: FEEDBACK
stations.title: Send intent; show only local feedback
stations.body: In StarterGui, create a ScreenGui named LaunchPadUI with a TextLabel named Status. Then put this LocalScript under StarterPlayerScripts. The client watches a local key press, asks the server to activate the pad, and changes only its own interface when the server replies. The label is feedback, never proof that the server accepted a reward.
stations.code:
local Players = game:GetService("Players")
local ReplicatedStorage = game:GetService("ReplicatedStorage")
local UserInputService = game:GetService("UserInputService")

local player = Players.LocalPlayer
local request = ReplicatedStorage:WaitForChild("LaunchPadRequest")
local status = player:WaitForChild("PlayerGui")
    :WaitForChild("LaunchPadUI")
    :WaitForChild("Status")

UserInputService.InputBegan:Connect(function(input, gameProcessed)
    if gameProcessed or input.KeyCode ~= Enum.KeyCode.F then
        return
    end
    request:FireServer("activate")
end)

request.OnClientEvent:Connect(function(message)
    status.Text = message
end)
checks: The LocalScript is in StarterPlayerScripts; LaunchPadUI and its Status label are in StarterGui.
Pressing F sends only the fixed activate intent, not a position, reward, or arbitrary Instance.
Near LaunchPad, the server response changes my local Status label.
Away from LaunchPad, the server response tells me to move closer.
I can explain why changing the label is local feedback, not a game-state grant.

stations.label: PROVE
stations.title: Test the boundary with more than one client
stations.body: Run a Studio server-and-clients playtest with at least two players. Confirm each client sees only its own local Status feedback, then repeat the request while outside the allowed distance. Finally add non-zero inbound and outbound Network Simulation delay in Studio settings and observe that the design remains understandable: a request can arrive later, but the server still decides the result.
checks: I ran a Studio server-and-clients test with at least two players.
Each client received only its own feedback; no client directly changed another client's UI.
The server rejected the request when the requesting player was too far from LaunchPad.
I repeated the test with non-zero simulated inbound and outbound network delay.
I recorded one observed result and one next improvement instead of claiming the test proved every network condition.

stations.label: MODEL
stations.title: Name the in-session state before changing it
stations.body: The next system tracks one server-owned fact: how many accepted LaunchPad activations this player has during this server session. The state contract is deliberately small: it starts at 0 when the Player joins, increases only after the server accepts a request, survives a character respawn, and disappears when the Player leaves. It is not saved, purchased, or trusted from the client.
checks: I can name the state: accepted LaunchPad activations for one Player in one server session.
I can name the owner: the server-side sessionState table.
I can name the transitions: join creates, accepted request increments, leave deletes.
I know a respawn replaces a character, not the Player's in-session state.
I do not call this progress persistence or a currency system.

stations.label: LIFECYCLE
stations.title: Create state once; handle every character
stations.body: Replace the previous server Script with this version. PlayerAdded creates one server-owned record. CharacterAdded handles every new avatar model. The existing-player loop prevents a timing gap if the Script starts after a Player is already present. Character state is temporary; player session state belongs to the Player record.
stations.code:
local Players = game:GetService("Players")
local ReplicatedStorage = game:GetService("ReplicatedStorage")
local Workspace = game:GetService("Workspace")

local request = ReplicatedStorage:WaitForChild("LaunchPadRequest")
local launchPad = Workspace:WaitForChild("LaunchPad")
local sessionState = {}
local COOLDOWN_SECONDS = 1
local MAX_DISTANCE = 12

local function onCharacterAdded(player, character)
    local humanoid = character:WaitForChild("Humanoid")
    print(player.Name .. " spawned with " .. humanoid.ClassName)
end

local function setUpPlayer(player)
    if sessionState[player] then
        return
    end

    sessionState[player] = {launches = 0, lastRequest = 0, revision = 0, dirty = false}
    player.CharacterAdded:Connect(function(character)
        onCharacterAdded(player, character)
    end)
    if player.Character then
        onCharacterAdded(player, player.Character)
    end
end

Players.PlayerAdded:Connect(setUpPlayer)
for _, player in Players:GetPlayers() do
    setUpPlayer(player)
end
checks: I use the Player object as the key for one server-owned session record.
The setup guard prevents duplicate records and duplicate CharacterAdded connections.
I handle a current character as well as future CharacterAdded events.
A respawn prints a new character lifecycle message without resetting launches.

stations.label: COMMIT
stations.title: Change state only after validation
stations.body: Add this handler below the lifecycle setup in the same server Script. The state is read only after the request passed type, context, distance, and rate checks. The player attribute is a server-published observation for Studio inspection or presentation; sessionState remains the authority and the server never reads a client claim back as truth.
stations.code:
request.OnServerEvent:Connect(function(player, action)
    local state = sessionState[player]
    if not state or typeof(action) ~= "string" or action ~= "activate" then
        return
    end

    local character = player.Character
    local root = character and character:FindFirstChild("HumanoidRootPart")
    if not root or (root.Position - launchPad.Position).Magnitude > MAX_DISTANCE then
        request:FireClient(player, "Move closer to LaunchPad")
        return
    end

    local now = time()
    if now - state.lastRequest < COOLDOWN_SECONDS then
        return
    end

    state.lastRequest = now
    state.launches += 1
    player:SetAttribute("Launches", state.launches)
    request:FireClient(player, "Accepted launches: " .. state.launches)
end)

Players.PlayerRemoving:Connect(function(player)
    sessionState[player] = nil
end)
checks: The handler rejects a player with no server session record.
launches increases only after the server accepts the request.
The server writes the Launches attribute after updating its authoritative record.
PlayerRemoving deletes the in-session record and does not try to save it.
I can explain why an attribute is an observation here, not the authority.

stations.label: BREAK
stations.title: Prove what survives and what does not
stations.body: In a Studio server-and-clients playtest, activate LaunchPad twice, reset the character, then activate it once more. The server-reported count should continue across the respawn. Leave and rejoin: the count must start again because this is intentional in-session state. This is the exact moment to learn the persistence boundary—not to paste in a DataStore.
checks: I observed accepted launches increase during one server session.
After a character reset, the next accepted activation continued the same player session count.
After leaving and rejoining, the count started over as designed.
I can state one failure case that must be designed before persistence, such as a failed save or duplicate award.
I did not add a DataStore merely because the count reset.

stations.label: CONTRACT
stations.title: Define saved data before opening storage
stations.body: Persistence starts with a tiny data contract, not a service call. For LaunchPad, the only proposed saved value is an integer launches count. A schema version tells future code how to interpret the record. Normalize untrusted or old stored data into a safe default before it enters your session state. This function is pure: it does no network work and can be tested without Studio API access.
stations.code:
local DEFAULT_DATA = {
    version = 1,
    launches = 0,
}

local function normalizeData(raw)
    if typeof(raw) ~= "table" then
        return {version = DEFAULT_DATA.version, launches = DEFAULT_DATA.launches}
    end

    local launches = raw.launches
    if typeof(launches) ~= "number" or launches ~= launches or launches < 0 then
        launches = DEFAULT_DATA.launches
    end

    return {
        version = DEFAULT_DATA.version,
        launches = math.floor(launches),
    }
end
checks: I can write the exact v1 record shape without including UI objects, Instances, or client-supplied tables.
Missing or malformed data becomes a safe default instead of crashing the session.
The data version is part of the record, not an assumption hidden in code.
I can explain why normalizeData is testable before any DataStore call exists.

stations.label: SNAPSHOT
stations.title: Keep the remote out of the save path
stations.body: An accepted LaunchPad request changes in-session state only. Mark that state dirty, then let a server-owned persistence layer decide when to save a snapshot. This prevents a client from turning one RemoteEvent into arbitrary cloud writes and makes retry behavior independent of input spam. A snapshot is plain data captured at one revision, not a reference to the live Player or character.
stations.code:
local function makeSnapshot(state)
    return {
        version = 1,
        launches = state.launches,
    }
end

local function markDirty(state)
    state.revision += 1
    state.dirty = true
end

-- Inside the already-validated server request handler:
state.launches += 1
markDirty(state)
-- Do not call a DataStore from this RemoteEvent handler.
checks: The RemoteEvent changes only validated server state and marks it dirty.
A snapshot contains only serializable plain data needed by the contract.
I can identify revision as the state version within this server session.
I do not put GetAsync, UpdateAsync, waits, or retry loops inside OnServerEvent.

stations.label: FAIL
stations.title: Choose a failure policy before writing
stations.body: Data store operations are network calls and can fail. Decide what the player experiences when a load fails, what remains unsaved after a save fails, how retries are scheduled outside the update callback, and when a session is safe to close. For this first system, a failed load must not silently look like a new account, and a failed save must remain visibly dirty for the server to retry or report.
checks: I can distinguish no saved record from a failed read request.
I can state whether a player may act before their data is confirmed loaded, and why.
I can state what dirty means after a failed save.
I know UpdateAsync callbacks must not yield, so retry waits belong outside the callback.
I can name one safe response to repeated save failure rather than pretending it succeeded.

sources.label: Roblox: Data stores — network failures and UpdateAsync

stations.label: TEST
stations.title: Use a safe persistence test boundary
stations.body: By default, Studio cannot access data stores. Enabling API access in Studio reaches the same stores as the client application, so never enable it against a live experience just to learn. First test normalization and dirty-state behavior with a mock repository. Only then use a separately published test version to exercise real server-side DataStore calls and inspect the result in Data Stores Manager.
checks: I tested normalizeData with nil, malformed, negative, and valid data without API access.
I can simulate a failed read or write in a mock without losing the in-session state contract.
I would use a separately published test version—not the live experience—before enabling Studio API access.
I know DataStoreService belongs in a server Script and a LocalScript cannot access it.

sources.label: Roblox: Data stores — Studio access safety

stations.label: LOAD
stations.title: Put reads behind a server-only repository
stations.body: Create a ModuleScript named PlayerDataService under ServerScriptService. This is the only place that talks to DataStoreService. A successful read with nil means there is no record yet; a failed read is a different state and must not silently become a new player. Retries surround the network call, never the UpdateAsync callback you will add next.
stations.code:
-- ServerScriptService/PlayerDataService
local DataStoreService = game:GetService("DataStoreService")
local store = DataStoreService:GetDataStore("LaunchPadData_v1")

local PlayerDataService = {}
local MAX_ATTEMPTS = 3

local function keyFor(player)
    return "player:" .. player.UserId
end

local function defaultData()
    return {version = 1, launches = 0}
end

local function normalizeData(raw)
    if typeof(raw) ~= "table" then
        return defaultData()
    end
    local launches = raw.launches
    if typeof(launches) ~= "number" or launches ~= launches or launches < 0 then
        launches = 0
    end
    return {version = 1, launches = math.floor(launches)}
end

function PlayerDataService.load(player)
    for attempt = 1, MAX_ATTEMPTS do
        local success, raw = pcall(function()
            return store:GetAsync(keyFor(player))
        end)
        if success then
            return true, normalizeData(raw), raw == nil
        end
        warn("Load failed for", player.UserId, "attempt", attempt, raw)
        if attempt < MAX_ATTEMPTS then
            task.wait(attempt)
        end
    end
    return false, nil, false
end

return PlayerDataService
checks: PlayerDataService is a ModuleScript under ServerScriptService, not a LocalScript or replicated module.
The data-store key comes from the server-provided UserId, not a client argument.
A successful nil read becomes default data; a failed pcall remains a failed load.
Retry waits occur after the pcall returns, never inside a data-store callback.

sources.label: Roblox: Data stores — server access and reads

stations.label: SAVE
stations.title: Save one immutable snapshot at a time
stations.body: Add this function to PlayerDataService. The caller captures a plain snapshot before calling save. Retrying the same snapshot writes the same desired record instead of incrementing it again, which is the first idempotency property you need. UpdateAsync is used because persistence may face multi-server attempts, but this tiny course system does not yet solve concurrent active-session ownership or merges—do not pretend it does.
stations.code:
function PlayerDataService.save(player, snapshot)
    local data = normalizeData(snapshot)

    for attempt = 1, MAX_ATTEMPTS do
        local success, result = pcall(function()
            return store:UpdateAsync(keyFor(player), function()
                -- Never yield in this callback. Return the immutable snapshot.
                return data
            end)
        end)
        if success then
            return true, normalizeData(result)
        end
        warn("Save failed for", player.UserId, "attempt", attempt, result)
        if attempt < MAX_ATTEMPTS then
            task.wait(attempt)
        end
    end
    return false, nil
end
checks: save receives a plain snapshot, never the live Player, Character, or sessionState table.
The UpdateAsync callback does not yield or call remote/UI code.
A retry writes the same normalized snapshot rather than applying another increment.
I can state the remaining design gap: concurrent active-server ownership needs an explicit later policy.

stations.label: SYNC
stations.title: Load before actions; clear dirty only for the saved revision
stations.body: Require PlayerDataService from the server Script. Start each Player as loading, refuse LaunchPad actions until a successful load produces a server record, and save only from a separate server task. If the state changes while a save is in flight, the revision differs and dirty stays true for a later snapshot. This protects newer in-session state from an older save completion.
stations.code:
local ServerScriptService = game:GetService("ServerScriptService")
local PlayerDataService = require(ServerScriptService:WaitForChild("PlayerDataService"))

local function saveIfDirty(player, state)
    if state.saving or not state.dirty then
        return
    end

    state.saving = true
    local revision = state.revision
    local snapshot = {version = 1, launches = state.launches}
    local success = PlayerDataService.save(player, snapshot)
    state.saving = false

    if success and state.revision == revision then
        state.dirty = false
    elseif not success then
        state.saveFailed = true
    end
end

-- In setUpPlayer, load before accepting actions:
state.loaded = false
task.spawn(function()
    local success, data = PlayerDataService.load(player)
    if not success or sessionState[player] ~= state then
        state.loadFailed = true
        return
    end
    state.launches = data.launches
    state.loaded = true
end)

-- In OnServerEvent, reject while loading or after a failed load:
if not state or not state.loaded or state.loadFailed then
    request:FireClient(player, "Data is unavailable; try again later")
    return
end
checks: The server does not accept an action before the player's load succeeds.
The save task captures revision and snapshot before the network call.
A successful old save cannot clear dirty after newer state changed.
A failed save leaves the server record dirty and marks a visible failure state for server handling.
No client decides when, what, or whether to persist.

stations.label: AUDIT
stations.title: Prove the reliability properties in a test version
stations.body: In a separately published test version, enable Studio API access only after mock tests pass. Test each result deliberately: new player gets the default record; a valid record loads; an intentionally simulated repository failure blocks activation instead of resetting data; two rapid accepted activations leave dirty true until the newest revision is saved; rejoin confirms the saved count. Inspect only the test version's records in Data Stores Manager.
checks: I tested default, valid, malformed, and simulated-failure load paths before enabling API access.
I used a separately published test version for real DataStore access.
I observed a failed load block activation instead of creating a silent replacement record.
I tested two accepted actions while a save was in flight and verified the newer revision stayed dirty.
I rejoined the test version and compared the loaded count with the last acknowledged saved snapshot.
I can name the remaining production concern: concurrent-server session ownership.

sources.label: Roblox: Data stores — Studio access safety

stations.label: LAYOUT
stations.title: Give each layer one home
stations.body: Before adding another feature, reorganize the LaunchPad project by ownership. Put the one server entry Script and server-only services in ServerScriptService; keep the narrow RemoteEvent in ReplicatedStorage; keep input/UI code in StarterPlayerScripts and StarterGui. A folder is not architecture by itself—the rule is that only the server entry point wires services to network events.
stations.code:
ServerScriptService
├── ServerMain              (Script · server entry point)
├── Services
│   ├── LaunchPadService     (ModuleScript)
│   └── PlayerDataService    (ModuleScript)

ReplicatedStorage
└── Remotes
    └── LaunchPadRequest     (RemoteEvent)

StarterPlayer
└── StarterPlayerScripts
    └── LaunchPadClient      (LocalScript)
checks: There is one ServerMain that owns wiring, not a collection of competing server Scripts.
PlayerDataService and LaunchPadService are server-only ModuleScripts.
The RemoteEvent is purpose-named and visible to both sides, but sensitive services are not replicated.
The LocalScript owns only client input and presentation.

stations.label: ENCAPSULATE
stations.title: Make one service own one game rule
stations.body: Create LaunchPadService as a ModuleScript under ServerScriptService/Services. It owns the rule for accepting a LaunchPad request. It receives the dependencies it needs instead of reaching through the whole game tree from every method. It does not require PlayerDataService, and PlayerDataService does not require it—this avoids circular modules and keeps persistence separate from gameplay validation.
stations.code:
-- ServerScriptService/Services/LaunchPadService
local LaunchPadService = {}
LaunchPadService.__index = LaunchPadService

function LaunchPadService.new(launchPad, maxDistance, cooldownSeconds)
    return setmetatable({
        launchPad = launchPad,
        maxDistance = maxDistance,
        cooldownSeconds = cooldownSeconds,
    }, LaunchPadService)
end

function LaunchPadService:canActivate(player, state, action)
    if not state.loaded or typeof(action) ~= "string" or action ~= "activate" then
        return false, "Data is unavailable; try again later"
    end
    local root = player.Character and player.Character:FindFirstChild("HumanoidRootPart")
    if not root or (root.Position - self.launchPad.Position).Magnitude > self.maxDistance then
        return false, "Move closer to LaunchPad"
    end
    if time() - state.lastRequest < self.cooldownSeconds then
        return false, "Please wait"
    end
    return true
end

return LaunchPadService
checks: LaunchPadService answers one question: can this request be accepted now?
The module receives its world dependency and configuration explicitly.
The module returns a decision and message; it does not fire remotes, save data, or own UI.
No service pair requires each other recursively.

stations.label: COMPOSE
stations.title: Wire services in one visible entry point
stations.body: ServerMain is intentionally boring: it gets Instances, requires services once, creates the LaunchPadService, and connects the RemoteEvent. The entry point still owns the state transition, dirty marking, client reply, and eventual save scheduling. This makes a feature traceable from network request to decision to state change without hiding authority in a client module.
stations.code:
local ReplicatedStorage = game:GetService("ReplicatedStorage")
local ServerScriptService = game:GetService("ServerScriptService")
local Workspace = game:GetService("Workspace")

local Services = ServerScriptService:WaitForChild("Services")
local LaunchPadService = require(Services:WaitForChild("LaunchPadService"))
local request = ReplicatedStorage:WaitForChild("Remotes"):WaitForChild("LaunchPadRequest")
local launchPad = Workspace:WaitForChild("LaunchPad")
local launchPads = LaunchPadService.new(launchPad, 12, 1)

request.OnServerEvent:Connect(function(player, action)
    local state = sessionState[player]
    local accepted, message = launchPads:canActivate(player, state, action)
    if not accepted then
        request:FireClient(player, message)
        return
    end

    state.lastRequest = time()
    state.launches += 1
    markDirty(state)
    request:FireClient(player, "Accepted launches: " .. state.launches)
    task.spawn(saveIfDirty, player, state)
end)
checks: ServerMain requires each service once and connects the RemoteEvent once.
The service decides validity; ServerMain performs the state transition only after approval.
saveIfDirty remains a server task, not a RemoteEvent implementation detail.
I can trace one request from LaunchPadClient through ServerMain to the service decision and back.

stations.label: OWNERSHIP
stations.title: Choose who may write before resolving conflicts
stations.body: A retry-safe snapshot is not a multi-server ownership policy. Before shipping persistent progression, choose an explicit rule: deny a second active session, transfer ownership safely, or design a mergeable record. MemoryStoreService is for fast, temporary cross-server coordination; DataStoreService is durable persistence. Do not use a client signal, a UI label, or a last-write-wins accident as your ownership system.
checks: I can explain why two active servers can overwrite each other's snapshots without an ownership policy.
I can choose and document one policy for this game before enabling persistent progression.
I know MemoryStoreService is ephemeral coordination, not durable player progress.
I will test expired ownership, server crash, reconnect, and handoff before calling the system production-safe.
